next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

By eleven the apartment no longer smelled like theirs. The building still leaked its ordinary hallway odor under the door, frying onions, damp cardboard, charger heat from delivery bikes stacked downstairs, but inside 5E somebody had laid a second atmosphere over everything. Citrus oil. Linen spray. A pale expensive cold that did not belong to their radiator or their budget. The diffuser in the living room exhaled on schedule from a matte white pod that had arrived with no return address, as if scent itself could be requisitioned into witness service.

Nita stood at the edge of the galley kitchen in stockings that kept slipping at the heel and watched a camera glide past Leela's place at the table. The camera operator did not touch anything. He did not have to. The apartment had been cleared into obedience over the last thirty-six hours by delivery windows, styling notes, remote prompts, and two different employer teams who kept calling the process an opportunity. Their chipped cereal bowls had been stacked inside the oven because the open shelves now held sponsored stoneware in the approved sand-and-cloud palette. The fridge was full in a way that felt accusatory: expensive yogurt in glass jars, blood oranges with branded produce stickers still on, a tray of herbs still in their hydroponic foam like roots being punished for staying alive.

Her blouse was Claude Atlantic blue-white, a shade calculated to flatter authority without admitting the body underneath it sweated. Sweat was collecting anyway, first in the notch of her spine, then under the band of the b...
open_hooks_to_carry: The open-house prep must feel invasive in domestic detail: surfaces, smell, wardrobe, script notes, fridge contents.
Leela refuses participation in a way that is child-plain rather than theatrically rebellious.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...points. Window timing. Surface guidance. Family-journey talking points. Recommended pantry edits. A note that child participation was "strongly preferred."

For a second neither of them spoke. The kitchen was all objects again: fruit bowl, coffee rings, the purple thermos still waiting on the top shelf, the place on the floor where his missing shirt button had been retrieved before sunrise. Everything in the room already looked half translated into exhibit copy.

Nita sat down across from him very carefully, one hand flattening the email as if paper could have slowed it, even though it lived on glass. "Well," she said. Her voice was flat now, beyond anger. "There's the day."

Ben set his phone down. His knees hurt. His mouth still remembered her. The showcase requirements scrolled on, patient and obscene.

"We should call Mina before Leela sees anything else," he said.

"Yes."

"And we need to decide whether we let anyone in here before we know the full terms."

"Yes."

He looked at her, trying one last time to find a human sentence that was not strategy and not salesmanship. Nothing arrived in usable form.

Nita reached for a pen from the junk drawer, the same drawer that still held the torn edge of last night's foil packet. She pulled a takeout menu aside, found the back of an old utility bill, and started making a list.

Ben stared at the sink mat, still a fraction crooked under the radiator side of the kitchen. The room already looked almost ready for cameras. That was the problem. What had happened on that floor had not changed the plan, only moved it under their skin.
state_deltas_required: The systems have decided to showcase the household, so every private object and anecdote is being converted into exhibit material before the family has found a way out.
The family reaches a point where compliance would amount to self-erasure, and Leela refuses to be part of the performance.
